claim: CA simulation results illustrate that the stress softening following peak stress after transiently increasing the Zener-Hollomon parameter ZP is attributed to the refinement of matrix and first-stage DRX grains (DRXGs) resulting from dislocation differences.
evidence_text: CA simulation results illustrate that the stress softening following peak stress after transiently increasing the Zener-Hollomon parameter ZP is attributed to the refinement of matrix and first-stage DRX grains (DRXGs ) resulting from dislocation differences.

claim: After decreasing ZP, even if the DRX fraction increases to levels under constant conditions, some matrix still exhibits higher dislocation density due to an inhomogeneous-dislocation-density distribution, which accelerates DRXGs growth to a size similar to that under the constant condition, producing a stress-decreasing rate that closely matches experimental findings.
evidence_text: After decreasing ZP, even if the DRX fraction increases to levels under constant conditions, some matrix still exhibits higher dislocation density due to an inhomogeneous-dislocation-density distribution. This accelerates DRXGs growth to a size similar to that under the constant condition, producing a stress-decreasing rate that closely matches experimental findings.

claim: Low-alloy steel samples were heated to 1250 °C and held for 180 s, compressed to a true strain of 0.9, held for 60 s, then water quenched, and the side section was examined by optical and EBSD microscopy.
evidence_text: Holding 180s at 1250°C ... Compressing ... Holding 60s ... Water quenching ... Side section ... Optical observation ... EBSD observation

claim: The 2D CA model by Zhang et al. (2022) did not address the differences in peak stress and steady-state stress under varying and constant deformation conditions.
evidence_text: these 3D CA models were calibrated under constant deformation conditions and lacked a unified description of flow stress and microstructure evolution under varying deformation conditions. Although Zhang et al. (2022) constructed a 2D CA model ... the differences in peak stress and steady-state stress under the varying and constant deformation conditions were not addressed.

claim: Specimens were heated to 1250 °C at 10 °C/s, held for 180 s to homogenize the austenite phase, then cooled to the deformation temperature at 10 °C/s and held for 60 s to reduce temperature fluctuations.
evidence_text: heated up to 1 2 5 0 °C at a rate of 1 0 °C / s and held for 180 s to homogenize the austenite phase. The temperature was then cooled to the corresponding deformation temperature at a rate of 1 0 °C / s and kept for 60 s to reduce the temperature fluctuations.

claim: Hot compression tests under constant deformation conditions were performed at temperatures of 850–1150 °C and effective strain rates of 0.01–10 s^{-1}.
evidence_text: The mechanical tests under constant deformation conditions were carried out at temperatures in the range of 8 5 0 - 1 1 5 0 °C and effective strain rates in the span of 0 . 0 1 - 1 0 s^{-1}.

claim: Under varying strain rate conditions, the first stage deforms the sample to ε_I of 0.2 or 0.4 at 950 °C and ε̇_I = 0.01 s^{-1} or 0.1 s^{-1}; then the strain rate is changed to ε̇_II = 0.1 s^{-1} or 0.01 s^{-1} and deformation continues to a total strain of 0.9.
evidence_text: In the first stage, the sample was deformed to the fixed strains (εI) of 0.2 or 0.4 at the constant temperature ( 9 5 0 °C ) and strain rate ( ε̇ I = 0 . 0 1 s^{-1} o r 0 . 1 s^{-1} ). The strain rate was then transiently increased or decreased by the indenter, and the specimen was compressed to a final strain ( ε t o t a l ) of 0.9 at a new strain rate ( ε̇ I I = 0 . 1 s^{-1} o r 0 . 0 1 s^{-1} ).

claim: Under varying temperature conditions at a strain rate of 0.01 s^{-1}, the temperature is continuously changed to 1050 °C or 950 °C over strain intervals of 0.2–0.3 or 0.4–0.5.
evidence_text: Fig. 1c shows experimental schemes under varying temperature conditions at a strain rate of 0 . 0 1 s^{-1}. They were similar to the test under the varying strain rate condition, except that the temperature was continuously increased or decreased to the corresponding temperature ( T I I = 1 0 5 0 °C or 9 5 0 °C ) in the strain ranges of 0.2–0.3 or 0.4–0.5.

claim: For optical observation, deformed specimens were split axially, and the central side section was ground, polished with 1 μm and 0.25 μm diamond pastes, and chemically etched with saturated picric acid solution.
evidence_text: Before performing the optical observation using an Olympus-GX71 optical microscope, these sections were ground with SiC papers, and polished with 1 μm and 0 . 2 5 μm diamond pastes, and then chemically etched using an abluent solution of saturation picric acid.

claim: Recrystallized and deformed grains were distinguished using GOS values with a threshold of 3° in AZtecCrystal software.
evidence_text: The reconstructed austenite EBSD maps were then applied to distinguish recrystallized grains from deformed ones from the AZtecCrystal software according to GOS values... and the threshold was defined at 3◦

claim: The differential form of recovery stress σ_v proposed by Zeng et al. (2019) describes work hardening as the first term and dynamic recovery as the second term in Eq. (1), with initial hardening rate θ_0 = μ/K_T.
evidence_text: Zeng et al. (2019) proposed the differential form of the recovery stress ... which was written as: ... where the first term means WH and the second term means DRV; θ_0 represents the initial WH rate

claim: The temperature-dependent shear modulus μ is given by μ = μ_0 (1 - 0.91((T - 300)/T_m)), where μ_0, K_{T0} and Q_{KT} are material constants.
Yield stress σ_0 and steady-state stress σ_ss are described by the Sellars-Tegart-Garofalo (STG) model with Zener-Hollomon parameters Z_0 = ε̇ exp(Q_0/RT) and Z_ss = ε̇ exp(Q_ss/RT), respectively.
evidence_text: σ_0 = σ_a + σ_{η0} sinh^{-1}[(Z_0/A_0)^{m_0}] and Z_0 = ε̇ exp(Q_0/RT) ; σ_ss = σ_{ηss} sinh^{-1}[(Z_ss/A_ss)^{m_ss}] and Z_ss = ε̇ exp(Q_ss/RT)

claim: Saturation stress σ_s follows the STG model as σ_s = σ_{η s} sinh^{-1}[(Z_p/A_s)^{m_s}] with Z_p = ε̇ exp(Q_p/RT), where Q_p is the deformation activation energy at peak stress.
evidence_text: σ_s = σ_{η s} sinh^{-1}[(Z_p/A_s)^{m_s}] and Z_p = ε̇ exp(Q_p/RT)

claim: DRX initiation is assumed when σ_v ≥ σ_c, where critical stress σ_c = k_c σ_s and k_c = A_c {ln[ε̇ exp(Q_c/RT)]}^{m_c} is a proportion parameter smaller than 1.
evidence_text: DRX could happen if the following condition was satisfied: σ_v ≥ σ_c ; σ_c = k_c σ_s ; k_c = A_c {ln[ε̇ exp(Q_c/RT)]}^{m_c}

claim: The time for 50% DRX fraction under constant deformation is t_0.5 = ψ Z_p^{-q} exp(Q_drx/RT), where ψ, q and Q_drx are material constants.
evidence_text: the time for a 50% DRX fraction was generally employed... which could be defined as: t_0.5 = ψ Z_p^{-q} exp(Q_drx/RT)

claim: To incorporate deformation history, the Zener-Hollomon parameter in t_0.5 is redefined as Z = A[sinh(ασ)]^n instead of Z_p from Eq. (6), so that t_0.5 evolves with transient stress.
evidence_text: a definition of the Zener-Hollomon parameter Z related to the stress evolution was used as follows: Z = A[sinh(ασ)]^n ; Eq. (11) was used instead of Z_p in Eq. (6) to calculate t_0.5

claim: The DRX fraction φ_d evolves according to a differential Avrami equation, Eq. (12), where dφ_d/dε depends on ε̇, t_0.5, Avrami exponent n_Av, and the current φ_d.
evidence_text: the equation of DRX fraction φ_d evolution in the differential form of the Avrami equation could be adjusted as: ... dφ_d/dε = (ln k_d/(ε̇ t_0.5^{n_Av})) n_Av (1-φ_d) [...]

claim: The total flow stress differential dσ/dε is given by Eq. (14): if σ_v < σ_c only WH+DRV contribute; if σ_v ≥ σ_c the DRX softening term (σ_s - σ_ss) dφ_d/dε is subtracted.
evidence_text: dσ/dε = dσ_v/dε, if σ_v < σ_c ; dσ/dε = dσ_v/dε - (σ_s - σ_ss)dφ_d/dε, if σ_v ≥ σ_c

claim: Eq. (15) modifies Eq. (14) by adding a net change in yield stress Δσ_0 to the recovery stress differential to reflect the effect of transient conditions on initial dislocation slip resistance.
evidence_text: Liu et al. (2023b) noted that the resistance to initial slip of dislocations... was influenced by transient condition changes and should be reflected... using the net change of the yield stress Δσ_0. Therefore, Eq. (14) was modified as follows: ... + Δσ_0

claim: The saturation stress and steady-state stress in the second stage are severely influenced by the first-stage DRX state, and the difference σ_s^(v) - σ_ss^(v) is equal to σ_s - σ_ss.
evidence_text: this paper used a similar method to establish the stress relationship between constant and varying deformation conditions as follows: σ_s^(v) - σ_ss^(v) = σ_s - σ_ss (16)

claim: A stress softening coefficient k_s (k_s ≤ 1) is introduced to define the saturation stress under varying deformation conditions; the saturation stress increment is scaled by k_s depending on whether the constant-condition saturation stress increases or decreases.
evidence_text: a stress softening coefficient $k _ { s }$ $( k _ { s } \le 1 )$ was used to define the saturation stress under the varying condition as follows: ... (17)

claim: The stress softening coefficient k_s is expressed as a function of the first-stage Z_P, second-stage Z_P, first-stage DRX fraction φ_d^(I), and a material constant n_c (Eq. 18).
evidence_text: the stress softening coefficient $k _ { s }$ was defined as follows: k_s (ε+Δε) = ( (ln Z_P^(I) + ln Z_P^(II)(ε+Δε)) / (2 Max(ln Z_P^(I), ln Z_P^(II)(ε+Δε))) )^{ n_c φ_d^(I) sqrt((ln Z_P^(II)(ε+Δε) - ln Z_P^(I))^2) / ln Z_P^(I) } (18)

claim: k_s converges to 1 when the first-stage DRX fraction approaches zero or when the second-stage Z_P equals the first-stage Z_P, recovering constant-condition behavior.
evidence_text: lim _ {\varphi_ {d} ^ {(I)} \rightarrow 0} k _ {s} \rightarrow 1 ; lim _ {\ln Z _ {p} ^ {(I I)} (\varepsilon + \Delta \varepsilon) \rightarrow \ln Z _ {p} ^ {(I)}} k _ {s} \rightarrow 1

claim: The work-hardening contribution to the dislocation density increment of a cell is given by dρ_WH^r = C dρ_WH (d_r/d_ave) [ (1/k_bd^r) Σ (L_p^r/L_0)^{-2} + rand(i,j,k) ] (Eq. 28), where the distance to grain boundaries and a random term control spatial heterogeneity.
evidence_text: dρ_WH^r(i,j,k) = C dρ_WH (d_r/d_ave) [ 1/k_bd^r ∑_{p=1}^{k_bd^r} (L_p^r(i,j,k)/L_0)^{-2} + rand(i,j,k) ] (28)

claim: The net evolution of any cell's dislocation density resulting from WH and dynamic recovery is dρ^r = dρ_WH^r - k_2 ρ^r (Eq. 29), where k_2 is the average DRV coefficient.
evidence_text: the evolution of any cell’s dislocation density resulting from WH and DRV was written as dρ^r(i,j,k) = dρ_WH^r(i,j,k) - k_2 ρ^r(i,j,k) (29)

claim: The grain-boundary sliding strain rate is expressed as ε̇_GBS^r = (A_GB H_GB μ b / KT) (b/d^r)^p_GB (σ_0^(v)/μ)^n_GB, with H_GB = H_0 exp(-E/RT) (Eq. 30), incorporating grain size and diffusion effects.
evidence_text: ε̇_GBS^r(i,j,k) = (A_GB H_GB μ b / K T) (b/d^r)^{p_GB} (σ_0^(v)/μ)^{n_GB} ; H_GB = H_0 exp(-E/RT) (30)

claim: The critical dislocation density for DRX nucleation is given by ρ_c = ((k_c - 1) σ_0^(ν) + k_c α_dis μ b √ρ_sat) / (α_dis μ b))^2 (Eq. 33).
evidence_text: After substituting Eq. (25) into Eq. (7), the critical dislocation density (ρ_c) could be described as ρ_c = ((k_c - 1) σ_0^(ν) + k_c α_dis μ b √ρ_sat) / (α_dis μ b))^2 (33)

claim: The migration velocity of DRX grain boundaries is v = M f, with driving force f = τ Δρ - 4γ/d_r, combining dislocation density difference and grain curvature (Eqs. 37–38).
evidence_text: The growth of DRX grains was influenced by the migration velocity of grain boundaries, and could be expressed as v = M f (37) where f represents the driving force of spherical recrystallized grains, and is described as f = τ Δρ - 4γ/d_r (38)

claim: Grain boundary energy γ equals γ_m for misorientation θ_i ≥ 10°, while for θ_i < 10° it follows γ = γ_m (θ_i/θ_m)[1 - ln(θ_i/θ_m)], where γ_m = (μ b θ_m) / (4π(1-v)) (Eqs. 39–40).
The CA time step is modified from Δt = L0/(ρ_sat τ M) because local dislocation density may exceed the average saturation value, ensuring the migration distance equals cell size.
evidence_text: However, due to the inhomogeneous dislocation density, the local dislocation density might be higher than saturation value ρ_sat of the average dislocation density. Eq. (42) represents the minimal time required for the migration distance of the DRX grain boundary to be equal to the size of a cell. Therefore, the above time step was modified as

claim: Table 1 lists the material parameters: μ0 = 7.89×10^10 MPa, Tm = 1800 K, Q_drx = 326,922 J/mol, Q_p = 314,300 J/mol, n = 6.3742, k_d0 = 4.442, n_c = 55.563, and others.
evidence_text: Table 1 Material parameters in the constitutive model. μ0 (MPa) 7.89×10^10 Tm (K) 1800 Q_drx (J/mol) 326,922 Q_p (J/mol) 314,300 n 6.3742 k_d0 4.442 n_c 55.563

claim: Fig. 6 shows experimental and simulated DRX fraction versus time for deformation temperatures 850, 950, 1050, 1150°C at strain rates 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10 s⁻¹, demonstrating close agreement; for example at 850°C, 0.01 s⁻¹, fraction rises from 0.0 at 0.001 s to 0.8 at 100 s identically in experiment and simulation.

Fig. 7 presents experimental and simulated flow stress-strain curves under constant conditions at 850°C, 950°C, 1050°C, 1150°C for various strain rates, showing good agreement; at 850°C, 0.01 s⁻¹, flow stress increases from 70 to 175 MPa (true strain 0.0–0.8) with exact match.
evidence_text: True strain Experimental (850°C_0.01s⁻¹) Simulated (850°C_0.01s⁻¹) ... 0.0 70 70 ... 0.8 175 175

claim: The modified time step Δt in the 3D CA model is given by Δt = L0/(ρ_max τ M), where L0 is the cell size, ρ_max is maximum dislocation density, τ is a factor, and M is grain boundary mobility.
evidence_text: Δt = \frac {L _ {0}}{\rho_ {\max} \tau M} \tag {43}

claim: If the cell's dislocation density is greater than or equal to ρ_c and the generated random number is less than or equal to nucleation probability P_nuc = ṅ L0^3 Δt, the cell transitions into the DRX nucleus state.
evidence_text: During each time step, if the cell's dislocation density was greater than or equal to $ρ _ { c }$ and the generated random number was less than or equal to the nucleation probability $P _ { n u c } = \dot { n } L _ { 0 } { } ^ { 3 } Δt ,$ the cell would transition into the DRX nucleus state

claim: The representative volume element (RVE) for 3D CA DRX simulation was 550 µm × 550 µm × 550 µm and the cell size L0 was set to 2.5 µm.
evidence_text: The RVE of 550 µm × 550 µm × 550 µm was applied in the DRX simulation, and the size $L_{0}$ of a cell was set to 2.5 µm

claim: Figure 8 compares experimental and simulated flow stress evolution under transient strain rate variations at T=950°C for ε̇_I=0.01 s⁻¹ → ε̇_II=0.1 s⁻¹ and the reverse, each with ε₁=0.2 or 0.4; subfigures (a) and (c) use Model 1 while (b) and (d) use Model 2.
evidence_text: Fig. 8. Experimental and simulated evolution of flow stress with transient strain rate variations at different ε₁ : (a) and (b) T=950°C, ε̇_I=0.01 s⁻¹, ε̇_II=0.1 s⁻¹, ε_I=0.2 or 0.4; (c) and (d) T=950°C, ε̇_I=0.1 s⁻¹, ε̇_II=0.01 s⁻¹, ε_I=0.2 or 0.4. Here (a) and (c) correspond to the simulation using Model 1 while (b) and (d) correspond to the simulation using Model 2.

claim: Figure 9 shows experimental and simulated flow stress evolution under varying temperatures at ε̇=0.01 s⁻¹ for T_I=1050°C → T_II=950°C and the reverse, at ε₁=0.2 or 0.4; (a) and (c) use Model 1, (b) and (d) use Model 2.
evidence_text: Fig. 9. Experimental and simulated evolution of flow stress with varying temperatures at different ε : (a) and (b) ε̇=0.01 s⁻¹, T_I=1050°C, T_II=950°C, ε_I=0.2 or 0.4; (c) and (d) ε̇=0.01 s⁻¹, T_I=950°C, T_II=1050°C, ε_I=0.2 or 0.4. Here (a) and (c) correspond to the simulation using Model 1 while (b) and (d) correspond to the simulation using Model 2.

claim: Under varying deformation conditions, Model 1 achieved RMSE 6.4554 MPa, R² 0.8239, and AARE 7.36%, while Model 2 achieved better performance with RMSE 3.0399 MPa, R² 0.9507, and AARE 3.11%.
evidence_text: Table 3 The accuracy evaluation of flow stress using both models under varying deformation conditions. ... Model 1: RMSE (MPa) 6.4554, R² 0.8239, AARE 7.36%; Model 2: RMSE (MPa) 3.0399, R² 0.9507, AARE 3.11%.

claim: Under T=950°C, strain rate increase from 0.01 s⁻¹ to 0.1 s⁻¹ at ε₁=0.2, experimental and Model 1 flow stress rose from ~60 MPa at ε=0.1 to ~105 MPa at ε=0.3 and then remained nearly constant.
For T=950°C, strain rate decrease from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹ at ε₁=0.4, the experimental flow stress dropped from ~118 MPa at ε=0.3–0.4 to ~75 MPa after the change and remained low.
With ε̇=0.01 s⁻¹, temperature drop from 1050°C to 950°C at ε₁=0.2, the flow stress decreased from ~78 MPa at ε=0.2 to ~73 MPa at ε=0.4 and continued to decline gradually thereafter.
For temperature increase from 950°C to 1050°C at ε₁=0.4, the flow stress under Model 1 dropped from ~85 MPa at ε=0.2–0.3 to ~60 MPa and stabilized.

The CA loop compares total strain to the first-stage strain ε_I to detect transient condition changes; when a change occurs, the stress softening coefficient k_s (Eq. 18) modifies the saturation stress via Eq. (17), followed by recalculation of dislocation density evolution, nucleation, and grain boundary migration until the final strain.
evidence_text: Initially, the total strain was compared with the first-stage strain ε_I to determine whether the deformation condition varied transiently. If an instantaneous variation in the deformation condition occurred, the stress softening coefficient k_s , calibrated by the constitutive model in Eq. (18), was used to obtain the varying saturation stress in Eq. (17).

claim: The initial stress σ0, saturation stress σss, steady-state stress σs, and critical stress σc are expressed by hyperbolic-sine and logarithmic functions of strain rate and temperature (Eq. 44) with material constants derived from experimental data.
evidence_text: σ_0 = 1 7. 7 1 6 + 1 0 5. 4 0 8 sinh^{-1} \left\{\left[ \frac {\dot {\varepsilon} e x p \left(\frac {3 2 1 6 7 1}{R T}\right)}{4 . 9 6 \times 1 0 ^ {1 4}} \right] ^ {0. 1 4 6} \right\} \\ \sigma_ {s s} = 2 9 2. 8 4 8 sinh^{-1} \left\{\left[ \frac {\dot {\varepsilon} e x p \left(\frac {3 1 9 6 6 2}{R T}\right)}{1 . 6 7 \times 1 0 ^ {1 5}} \right] ^ {0. 1 7 0} \right\} \\ \sigma_ {s} = 1 6 0. 8 5 4 sinh^{-1} \left\{\left[ \frac {\dot {\varepsilon} e x p \left(\frac {3 1 4 3 0 0}{R T}\right)}{6 . 2 8 \times 1 0 ^ {1 3}} \right] ^ {0. 1 4 6} \right\} \\ \sigma_ {c} = 1 1 1. 6 6 1 \left\{\ln \left[ \dot {\varepsilon} e x p \left(\frac {3 7 6 4 7 5}{R T}\right) \right] \right\} ^ {0. 0 9 3} sinh^{-1} \left\{\left[ \frac {\dot {\varepsilon} e x p \left(\frac {3 1 4 3 0 0}{R T}\right)}{6 . 2 8 \times 1 0 ^ {1 3}} \right] ^ {0. 1 4 6} \right\}

claim: The experimental DRX fraction φ_d is obtained from flow stress as φ_d = (σ_v - σ) / (σ_s - σ_ss) (Eq. 45).
evidence_text: φ_d = (σ_v - σ) / (σ_s - σ_ss) (Eq. 45)

claim: At a high strain rate of 10 s⁻¹, DRX nucleation and growth are hindered; dislocation multiplication dominates, causing no significant initial stress softening, and interfacial friction later produces a slow initial increase followed by rapid flow stress growth.
evidence_text: At a high strain rate, DRX grains are difficult to undergo the nucleation and growth. The dislocation multiplication rate caused by WH plays a dominant role, resulting in no obvious stress softening in the initial stage. As deformation progresses, the increase in friction on the contact surface leads to a marked rise in flow stress. Consequently, the flow curve exhibits a trend of initial slow increase followed by rapid growth

claim: Model 1 does not modify the saturation stress and the time for 50% DRX t_0.5 (Eq. 10), whereas Model 2 incorporates a modified t_0.5 (Eq. 12) and a stress softening coefficient k_s (Eq. 18).
evidence_text: Model 1 represents a constitutive model that does not modify the saturation stress and t_0.5 (Eq. (10)), as proposed by Puchi-Cabrera et al. (2013, 2014). The constitutive model using modified t_0.5 and softening coefficient k (Eqs. (12) and (18)) is named Model 2.

claim: When the strain rate suddenly rises from 0.01 s⁻¹ (ε̇_I) to 0.1 s⁻¹ (ε̇_II), the experimental flow stress peak is lower than under constant conditions, and peak stress and steady-state stress decrease with increasing onset strain ε_I. Model 1 predicts similar steady-state stress regardless of ε_I, while Model 2 captures the lower steady-state stress due to the softening coefficient k_s.
evidence_text: when suddenly improving the strain rate from 0.01 s^{-1} (ε̇_I) to 0.1 s^{-1} (ε̇_II), the experimental stress gradually reached a lower peak compared to that observed under the constant deformation condition. … the greater the strain where the strain rate transiently increased, the lower the peak stress and steady-state stress in the second stage became. … Model 1, the steady-state stress predicted by Model 1 tended to be almost similar … Model 2 took the effect of the softening coefficient k_s on the characteristic stresses into account, the steady-state stress declined more significantly

claim: When the strain rate drops from 0.1 s⁻¹ to 0.01 s⁻¹, the experimental stress falls below the constant condition stress within ~0.04 strain interval; larger ε_I accelerates the drop. Model 1 delays softening and cannot predict a lower steady-state stress, whereas Model 2 accurately reproduces the rapid stress decrease.
evidence_text: when the strain rate transiently declined from 0.1 s^{-1} (ε̇_I) to 0.01 s^{-1} (ε̇_π), the experimental stress fell to a level lower than that of the constant deformation condition in the about 0.04 strain interval. The greater the strain where the strain rate transiently decreased, the faster the stress descended … Model 1 exhibited a remarkable delay in predicting the stress softening, while Model 2 proved to be more accurate … Model 1 could not predict a lower steady-state stress than that of the constant deformation condition

claim: When the temperature decreases from 1050°C to 950°C within 0.1 strain, WH strengthening raises stress, and a second-stage peak lower than under constant conditions appears; the peak stress decreases with larger ε_I. Model 1 underestimates stress softening, while Model 2 shows higher prediction accuracy.
evidence_text: when the temperature decreases from 1050 °C (T_I) to 950 °C (T_II) within a 0.1 strain interval, the strengthening WH brought an increase in experimental stress. And at the end of the temperature-decreasing process, a new peak lower than that of the constant deformation condition occurred … when the strain where the temperature started to decrease became greater, the peak stress level in the second stage was lower. … Model 1, the predicted stress softening was weaker … Model 2 showed a higher prediction accuracy

claim: When the temperature rises from 950°C to 1050°C over 0.1 strain, the experimental stress gradually decreases below the constant condition stress; larger ε_I produces stronger softening. Model 1 again delays the stress softening, whereas Model 2 accurately captures the softening evolution.
evidence_text: rising temperature from 950 °C (T_I) to 1050 °C (T_II) in the 0.1 strain interval resulted in the experimental stress gradually decreasing to a lower level lower than that of the constant deformation condition. … the greater the strain where the temperature began to increase, the stronger the softening effect … Model 1 still had a delay in the prediction of the stress softening … Model 2 showed a higher prediction accuracy

claim: Under varying deformation conditions, Model 2 exhibits a higher R² and lower RMSE and AARE compared to Model 1, indicating superior performance as shown in Table 3.
evidence_text: Table 3 shows the accuracy evaluation of flow stress using both models under varying deformation conditions. Compared to Model 1, Model 2 exhibited a higher R^2, as well as lower RMSE and AARE, indicating superior performance under varying conditions.

claim: Finite element force-stroke simulations under varying deformation conditions show that Model 1 predicts a slower force decrease in the second stage and significantly overestimates the force magnitude, while Model 2 better reproduces the experimental force evolution (Fig. 10).
evidence_text: Due to the delay in forecasting the decline in flow stress following an abrupt decrease in strain rate or a temperature rise using Model 1 … the decrease velocity of the predicted force in the second stage was obviously lower than experimental results. Moreover, it can be observed that the second-stage force forecasted by Model 1 significantly exceeded the experimental curves, while Model 2 better captured the evolution of the force

claim: The 3D CA model uses material parameters: K=1.38×10⁻²³ J/K, C_drx=1.99×10¹⁰, b=2.45×10⁻¹⁰ m, ν=0.33, E=305,200 J/mol, δD_ob=5×10⁻¹⁰ m³/s, A_GB=37.5, H₀=2.63×10⁻⁷, P_GB=2, n_GB=2, V_m=7.18×10⁻⁶ m³/mol, α_dis=0.5, as listed in Table 4.
evidence_text: K (J/K) 1.38×10^{-23} Eq. (30) Liu et al. (2024) C_drx 1.99×10^{10} Eq. (35) Appendix B1 b (m) 2.45×10^{-10} Eq. (30) Xu et al. (2023) v 0.33 Eq. (40) Wu et al. (2020) E (J/mol) 305,200 Eq. (30) Appendix B1 δD_ob (m3/s) 5×10^{-10} Eq. (41) Zhang et al. (2016b) A_GB 37.5 Eq. (30) Appendix B1 H_0 2.63×10^{-7} Eq. (30) Appendix B1 P_GB 2 Eq. (30) Appendix B1 n_GB 2 Eq. (30) Kocks and Mecking (2003) V_m 7.18×10^{-6} Eq. (36) Zheng et al. (2009) α_dis 0.5 Eq. (25) Zhang et al. (2016b)

claim: The parameters C_drx, E, A_GB, H_O, and P_GB of the 3D CA model were determined using a genetic algorithm (GA) optimization method, with the optimized values presented in Table 4.
evidence_text: The values of C_drx, E, AGB, H_O and P_GB were solved by the GA optimization method. ... as shown in Table 4.

claim: During 3D CA simulation at 850°C and 0.01 s^-1, higher distortion energy at large grain boundaries caused preferential DRX nucleation, and flow stress began to fall at 0.45 strain; the final steady state was reached at 0.9 strain due to balance between dislocation annihilation and generation.
evidence_text: In the initial stage, the higher distortion energy (Eq. (36)) caused by the strain concentration at the grain boundaries of large grains led to the preferential nucleation of DRX grains (Eq. (35)). ... so at 0.45 strain, the flow stress started to fall. ... hence progressively bringing the stress into a steady state at 0.9 strain.

claim: At a higher strain rate of 1 s^-1 compared to 0.01 s^-1, DRX grains experienced greater difficulty in nucleation and growth due to increasing critical dislocation density ρ_c and decreasing deformation time, causing more matrix to remain on the 3D CA model surfaces.
evidence_text: the DRX grains experienced greater difficulty in nucleation and growth at 1 s^-1 due to the increasing ρ_c and decreasing deformation time. And the matrix finally remained more on the surfaces of a 3D CA model, as shown in Fig. 11.

claim: Under constant deformation at 850°C and 0.01 s^-1, the 3D CA simulation gave an average grain size of 34.53 μm and a DRX fraction of 0.8 at a true strain of 1.0.
evidence_text: | True strain | Average grain size(3D CA) (μm) | DRX fraction(3D CA) (μm) | ... | 1.0 | 34.53 | 0.8 |

claim: Under constant deformation at 850°C and 1 s^-1, the 3D CA simulation gave an average grain size of 50 μm and a DRX fraction of 0.8 at a true strain of 1.0.
evidence_text: | True strain | ... DRX fraction(3D CA) (μm) | ... | 1.0 | 50 | 0.8 |

claim: The simulated section and 3D microstructures showed differences in AGS and DRX fraction because of uneven refinement of the initial mixed-grain microstructure; decreasing strain rate to 0.01 s^-1 reduced this gap.
evidence_text: The simulated section and 3D microstructures had some differences in the AGS and DRX fraction due to the uneven refinement of the mixed-grain microstructure under the above deformation conditions. When decreasing the strain rate to 0.01 s^-1, the matrix was refined more, and the gap between section and 3D simulation results became lower.

claim: At 950°C, when strain rate was decreased from 0.1 s^-1 to 0.01 s^-1 at ε1=0.2, the DRX fraction at ε=0.4 was 0.5, compared to 0.4 for ε1=0.4, and constant condition values of 0.8 (0.01 s^-1) and 0.6 (0.1 s^-1).
evidence_text: | True strain | DRX fraction (0.01s⁻¹(Constant)) | DRX fraction (0.1s⁻¹(Constant)) | DRX fraction (0.1s⁻¹-0.01s⁻¹(0.2 strain)) | DRX fraction (0.1s⁻¹-0.01s⁻¹(0.4 strain)) | ... | 0.4 | ~0.8 | ~0.6 | ~0.5 | ~0.4 |

claim: At a strain rate of 0.01 s^-1, when temperature was decreased from 1050°C to 950°C at ε1=0.2, the DRX fraction at ε=0.3 was 0.6, at ε=0.4 was 0.8; for ε1=0.4, it was 0.7 at ε=0.3 and 0.9 at ε=0.4, compared to constant 950°C (0.6 and 0.8) and 1050°C (0.8 and 0.9).
evidence_text: | True strain | DRX fraction (950°C Constant) | DRX fraction (1050°C Constant) | DRX fraction (950°C-1050°C(0.2 strain)) | DRX fraction (950°C-1050°C(0.4 strain)) | ... | 0.3 | 0.6 | 0.8 | 0.6 | 0.7 | ... | 0.4 | 0.8 | 0.9 | 0.8 | 0.9 |

claim: For a strain rate decrease from 0.1 to 0.01 s^-1 at ε1=0.2, the experimental average grain size (AGS) at ε=0.5 was 55 μm and 3D CA simulation gave 50 μm; at ε=0.9, both were 50 μm. For ε1=0.4, at ε=0.5, AGS was 50 μm (3D CA) and 55 μm (experiment), at ε=0.9 both were 50 μm.
For a temperature decrease from 1050 to 950°C at 0.01 s^-1 with ε1=0.2, experimental AGS at ε=0.5 was 75 μm, 3D CA gave 78 μm; at ε=0.9, 60 μm and 62 μm. For ε1=0.4, similar values were observed.
After transiently increasing the Zener-Hollomon parameter Zp, the 3D CA model effectively described the peak stress and subsequent stress decline in the second deformation stage.
evidence_text: It is clear that the CA model effectively described the peak stress and subsequent stress decline in the second stage after transiently increasing Zₚ in Fig. 13a and 13c.

claim: After transiently decreasing Zp, the predicted reduction velocity of flow stress from the CA model agreed better with experimental results compared to Model 2.
evidence_text: And the predicted reduction velocity of flow stress agreed better with experimental results compared to Model 2 after transiently decreasing Zₚ in Figs. 8d, 9d, 13b and 13d.

claim: In the CA model, after transiently increasing the strain rate the DRX fraction continued to increase because the DRX grains generated in the first stage could keep growing, despite a fall in nucleation caused by elevated critical dislocation density ρc.
evidence_text: In the CA model, although the increase in ρc due to the elevated strain rate led to a fall in the number of nucleation grains, the DRX fraction still kept increasing because the DRX grains generated in the first stage could continue to grow.

claim: After transiently increasing Zp, the DRX fraction growth rate at the start of the second stage was lower than under constant conditions due to limited nucleation, and the subsequent DRX fractions were all lower than the corresponding constant-condition values.
evidence_text: Compared to the corresponding simulation results under constant conditions, the DRX fraction's growth rate was lower at the start of the second stage because of the limitation of nucleation. Besides, although the DRX fraction evolutions obtained by both models differed in the early stage of varying strain rate, their subsequent DRX fractions were all lower than corresponding results under the constant conditions in the latter part of the second stage in Fig. 14a.

claim: After transiently decreasing Zp, the CA model exhibited a greater growth rate of DRX fraction at the beginning of the second stage compared to the constant condition, and the DRX fraction could eventually exceed the corresponding constant-condition value.
evidence_text: It can be observed that a decline in Zₚ could result in a greater growth rate of DRX fraction compared to the corresponding simulation result under the constant condition at the beginning of the second stage. Besides, it can be observed from Fig. 14b and 14d that the DRX fraction in the second stage simulated by CA could gradually exceed the corresponding findings under constant conditions.

claim: In Model 1, the time for 50% DRX (t0.5) is determined only by the temperature and strain rate of the current stage via Zp, causing the DRX fraction growth rate after a transient Zp decrease to remain nearly identical to that under constant conditions.
evidence_text: In Model 1, t0.5 was calculated according to Eq. (10) and mainly affected by Zₚ ... This implies that regardless of the softening effect in the first stage, t0.5 in the second stage could only be influenced by temperature and strain rate of that stage. This is why the simulated growth rate of the DRX fraction was almost consistent with that observed under the corresponding constant condition in Fig. 15.

claim: After transiently increasing Zp, the experimental AGS at 0.9 strain increased with rising ε1, as shown in Fig. 16a and 16c.
evidence_text: In Fig. 16a and 16c, it can be observed that the experimental AGS at 0.9 strain after transiently increasing Zp became greater with the rising ε1.

claim: After transiently decreasing Zp at different ε1, the final experimental AGSs were almost similar, as shown in Fig. 16b and 16d.
evidence_text: the final AGSs in experimental results were almost similar after transiently decreasing Zp at different ε1 in Fig. 16b and 16d.

claim: At 950 °C, when the strain rate was transiently increased, the AGS in the second stage at ε1=0.2 was slightly higher than that under constant condition at 0.9 strain, and the difference became greater as ε1 increased to 0.4 (Fig. 17a).
evidence_text: The reconstructed results in Fig. 17a indicate that the AGS in the second stage at 0.2 ε1 was slightly higher than the corresponding one under the constant condition at 0.9 strain. And as ε1 increased to 0.4, the difference between both AGSs became greater.

claim: The first-stage DRX grains (DRXGsI, marked green) finally remained more with rising ε1 (Fig. 17c, 17d, 17e).
evidence_text: the first-stage DRX grains (DRXGsI), marked as green, finally remained more with rising ε1.

claim: After suddenly increasing strain rate at 0.4 strain, the experimental microstructure began to refine further at 0.5 strain, but its AGS was still greater than the result at a constant strain rate of 0.1 s⁻¹.
evidence_text: In Fig. 17b, after suddenly increasing strain rate at 0.4 strain, the experimental microstructure began to refine further at 0.5 strain. However, its AGS was still greater than the result observed at a constant strain rate of 0.1 s⁻¹.

claim: The simulated AGS after transiently increasing strain rate at 0.4 strain was larger than that at a constant rate of 0.1 s⁻¹ at 0.5 strain, as newly formed red DRX grains gradually refined the matrix and DRXGsI, finally reaching a DRX fraction approaching 1.
evidence_text: Consequently, the simulated AGS after transiently increasing strain rate at 0.4 strain was larger than that at a constant rate of 0.1 s⁻¹ at 0.5 strain in Fig. 17c. As deformation progressed, the newly formed red recrystallization grains gradually refined the matrix and DRXGsI. Finally, the DRX fraction of simulated microstructure approached 1.

claim: A drop in temperature led to an increase in the final AGS compared to the results obtained at a constant temperature of 950 °C and 0.9 strain, while both DRX fractions were almost close, similar to findings at transiently rising strain rate (Fig. 19a).
evidence_text: In Fig. 19a, a drop in temperature led to an increase in the final AGS compared to the results obtained at a constant temperature of 950 °C and 0.9 strain while both DRX fractions were almost close, similar to experimental findings at the transiently rising strain rate.

claim: At the end of decreasing temperature (0.5 strain), the microstructure underwent a slight refinement, but the AGS remained significantly higher than that at a constant temperature of 950 °C and 0.5 strain (Fig. 19b).
evidence_text: Fig. 19b depicts that the microstructure underwent a slight refinement at the end of decreasing temperature (0.5 strain). Nevertheless, the AGS remained significantly higher than that at the constant temperature of 950 °C and 0.5 strain.

claim: The simulation results suggest that the final AGS after suddenly decreasing the temperature when ε1 was 0.4 exceeded that at the corresponding constant condition, matching experimental findings (Fig. 19a).
evidence_text: Moreover, the simulation results suggest that the final AGS after suddenly decreasing the temperature when ε1 was 0.4 exceeded that at the corresponding constant condition, similar to the experimental findings presented in Fig. 19a.

claim: A sudden decrease in strain rate caused the DRX grains generated during the first stage (950 °C, 0.1 s⁻¹) to grow rapidly; at 0.5 strain the matrix was almost completely refined, and the simulated AGS was slightly lower than that at a constant strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹ and 0.9 strain, matching experimental findings, and with rising strain the AGS fluctuated slowly to the constant level (Fig. 20b, Fig. 16b).
evidence_text: In Fig. 20b, a sudden decrease in strain rate caused the DRX grains generated during the first stage (950 °C and 0.1 s⁻¹) to grow rapidly. At 0.5 strain, the matrix was almost completely refined, and the AGS was slightly lower than the simulation results at the constant strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹ and 0.9 strain in Fig. 19c. This was consistent with the experimental findings in Fig. 20a. With the rising strain, the simulated AGS could slowly fluctuate to the level at the constant strain rate of 0.01 s⁻¹, as shown in Fig. 16b

claim: At the end of a temperature increase from 950 °C to 1050 °C in strain range 0.4–0.5 at 0.01 s⁻¹, the AGS decreased slightly from 65.33 µm at 0.4 strain to 62.63 µm at 0.5 strain (Fig. 21a).
evidence_text: From Fig. 17b and Fig. 21a, it can be seen that at the end of the temperature rise, the AGS decreased slightly from 65.33 µm at 0.4 strain (at 950 °C and 0.01 s⁻¹) to 62.63 µm at 0.5 strain.

claim: Under increasing temperature, the microstructure initially underwent refinement and then coarsening, resulting in nearly identical AGS at 0.5 and 0.4 strains, and the simulated AGS at 0.5 strain was lower than that at a constant temperature of 1050 °C due to the onset of coarsening (Fig. 16d, Fig. 21c, 21d).
evidence_text: Combined with the simulated evolution of AGS in Fig. 16d, it can be found that the microstructure initially underwent refinement and then coarsening, resulting in AGSs at 0.5 and 0.4 strains that were nearly identical. Besides, the simulation results indicate that the AGS at 0.5 strain under increasing temperature was lower than that at a constant temperature of 1050 °C due to the onset of microstructure coarsening.

claim: Under varying deformation conditions, the 3D CA model accuracy for flow stress, AGS, and DRX fraction is: flow stress RMSE=3.3118 MPa, R²=0.9589, AARE=4.57%; AGS RMSE=4.0169 µm, R²=0.9777, AARE=4.11%; DRX fraction RMSE=0.0229, R²=0.9784, AARE=2.47% (Table 5).
evidence_text: Table 5 presents the accuracy evaluation of flow stress, AGS and DRX fraction using 3D CA under varying deformation conditions. Flow stress (varying conditions) RMSE 3.3118 MPa R² 0.9589 AARE 4.57 %; AGS (varying conditions) RMSE 4.0169 μm R² 0.9777 AARE 4.11 %; DRX fraction (varying conditions) RMSE 0.0229 R² 0.9784 AARE 2.47 %

claim: DRXGsI can grow quickly to a nearly similar size as under constant condition within a very low strain interval after transiently decreasing Z_P, accelerating microstructure refinement.
evidence_text: the DRXGsI can grow quickly to an almost similar size under the corresponding constant condition in a very low strain interval after transiently decreasing Z _ { P } in Fig. 27c and ^ { 2 7 \mathrm { d } , } , accelerating the microstructure refinement in Fig. 14b and Fig. 14d.

claim: The updated phenomenological constitutive model, which accounts for varying time for 50% DRX and residual softening from the first stage, significantly improves flow stress prediction compared to the original model: R^2 increases from 0.82 to 0.95, RMSE decreases from 6.46 MPa to 3.04 MPa, and AARE drops from 7.36% to 3.11%.
evidence_text: Compared to the original one, the updated model shows a significant improvement, with R ^ { 2 } increasing from 0.82 to 0.95. Besides, the RMSE decreases from 6.46 MPa to 3.04 MPa, and the AARE drops from 7.36 % to 3.11 %.

claim: After transiently increasing Z_P, when the first-stage DRX fraction is very high, the significant dislocation density difference between stages causes DRX grains from the first stage to be refined by those nucleated in the second stage, resulting in stress softening similar to experimental findings.
evidence_text: when the DRX fraction in the first stage is very high, due to a significant difference in dislocation density between DRX grains generated in different stages after transiently increasing Z _ { P } , the DRX grains formed in the first stage are refined by those nucleated in the second stage, causing the stress softening similar to experimental findings.

claim: Under transient decrease of Z_P, the DRX fraction can exceed that under constant deformation conditions because greater dislocation density promotes growth of DRX grains in both stages, leading to more accurate prediction of stress decrease rate compared to the modified constitutive model.
evidence_text: The greater dislocation density promotes the growth of DRX grains in both stages. Consequently, the DRX fraction under the transient decrease of Z _ { P } can exceed that under the constant condition, leading to a more accurate prediction of the stress decrease rate compared to the simulation results using a modified constitutive model.

claim: Experimental results show that a transient increase in Z_P significantly increases the final average grain size (AGS) with rising ε1, while a transient decrease in Z_P has little effect on final AGS.
evidence_text: The experimental results show that a transient increase in Z _ { P } significantly increases the final AGS with rising \varepsilon _ { 1 } { _ { \mathrm { : } } } , while a transient decrease in Z _ { P } has little effect on the final AGS.

claim: CA simulations reveal that after transiently increasing Z_P, DRXGsI cannot be completely refined due to a delay in DRXGsII nucleation, and as ε1 increases, larger DRXGsI remain more, leading to greater final AGS.
evidence_text: after transiently increasing Z _ { P } , DRXGsI cannot be refined completely due to a delay in the DRXGsII nucleation. And as \varepsilon _ { 1 } increases, the larger DRXGsI remain more, resulting in a greater final AGS.

claim: After transiently decreasing Z_P, the remaining higher dislocation density allows DRX grains in both stages to grow successively to sizes nearly identical to those under corresponding constant conditions, keeping AGS unchanged.
evidence_text: After transiently decreasing Z _ { P } , the remaining higher dislocation density allows DRX grains in both stages to grow successively to sizes that are nearly identical to those under the corresponding constant conditions soon. Finally, the DRXGs remain low, and the AGS keeps the same.
